entries.content: I want to add a feature where when a snake loses it freezes showing how you lost until a key is pressed.

I'll explore the codebase to understand how the game handles losing, then figure out the best place to add this freeze-on-loss feature.

I'll explore the codebase to understand how losing/game-over is currently handled, the game loop, and input/rendering. Let me launch parallel Explore agents.

Both Explore agents are running in parallel. I'll wait for their findings before designing the plan.

I've launched both Explore agents and they're running in the background. I'll wait for their findings before designing the plan.

<summary>Agent "Explore rendering & input handling" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>I now have a complete picture. Here is my report.

## Summary

All code lives in `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/main.ts` (one big `SnakeGame` class), `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/index.html` (DOM screens), and `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/style.css` (styling). The `feature/lossreason` branch is currently identical to `main` (0 commits ahead, no uncommitted changes) â€” so there is no partial work yet, but a `deathCause` field already exists as a foundation (see section 4).

The single most important architectural fact for your feature: **the game never freezes on the canvas showing the death today.** On any death it immediately swaps to a separate DOM screen (`showScreen('game-over')`), which hides the whole `#game` element (canvas included), and the colliding head cell is never even drawn (collision is checked *before* the head is added to the snake). Your feature will need to change both of these.

---

## 1. Rendering

**Main draw function: `draw()` â€” `main.ts:563-629`.** Called on every game tick, on resize, on rotation, and on view toggle.

- Canvas cleared each frame by filling the whole canvas (`main.ts:564-566`):
```ts
const ctx = this.ctx
ctx.fillStyle = '#1a1a1a'
ctx.fillRect(0, 0, this.canvas.width, this.canvas.height)
```
- Then draws ground plane / grid lines / border: `drawGroundPlane()`, `drawGridLines()`, `drawGroundBorder()` (`main.ts:568-570`).
- Collects snake segments + food into an `objects[]` array (`main.ts:572-585`), sorts them back-to-front (painter's algorithm) at `main.ts:588-590`, draws shadows (`drawBlockShadow`, `main.ts:593-595`), then draws each block via `drawBlock(x, y, topColor, leftColor, rightColor)` in the `switch` at `main.ts:610-628`. Food is the red block (`#ef4444...`) at `main.ts:624-625`.
- Individual block geometry: `drawBlock` around `main.ts:441-500` (extruded isometric cube faces), `drawBlockShadow` around `main.ts:420-439`.
- The snake color also gets a "hunger tint" that lerps toward a warning palette as starvation nears (`main.ts:597-607`) â€” relevant precedent for signalling state visually.

**Overhead / top-down view toggle** (from git `93f7100 "Add overhead (top-down) view toggle"`): state field `overheadView: boolean` at `main.ts:139` (with saved iso settings at `main.ts:140-142`). Toggled by `toggleOverheadView()` at `main.ts:527-545` (bound to the `O` key at `main.ts:821-824`). It sets `isoAngle = 0`, `perspectiveRatio = 1`, `perspectiveStrength = 0`, then calls `updateCanvasSize()` + `draw()`. It affects rendering mainly via `baseBlockHeight` â€” in overhead mode blocks are flattened to squares with no extruded height (`main.ts:229-230`). Any overlay you draw on the canvas will work in both views since it's drawn on top after all blocks.

---

## 2. Existing overlays / text

**There is NO canvas text anywhere** â€” `grep` for `fillText`/`strokeText` in `src/` returns nothing. All text is DOM-based.

Relevant DOM screens in `index.html`:
- `#game` screen (`index.html:79-100`) contains the canvas. Note the canvas is wrapped in `.canvas-wrap` (`index.html:95-98`):
```html
<div class="canvas-wrap">
  <canvas id="canvas"></canvas>
  <div id="starvation-warning" class="starvation-warning hidden" role="alert"></div>
</div>
```
- `#game-over` screen (`index.html:101-123`): a separate full screen with `<h1 id="game-over-title">`, `#game-over-winner`, final score, high score, stats, leaderboard prompt, and `#play-again` / `#view-leaderboard` / `#back-to-menu` buttons.
- `#pause` overlay (`index.html:124-127`): `<h1>Paused</h1>` + "Press P to Resume".

**The `starvation-warning` element is your best pattern to copy for a canvas overlay.** It is a DOM element absolutely positioned *over* the canvas inside `.canvas-wrap` without shifting layout. CSS at `style.css:177-206`:
```css
.canvas-wrap { position: relative; display: inline-flex; }
.starvation-warning { position: absolute; top: 16px; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%); ... pointer-events: none; animation: starve-pulse ...; }
```
The `#pause` and `#game-over` full-screen overlays use `.screen.overlay` (`style.css:88-97`): `position: fixed; inset: 0; background: rgba(0,0,0,0.85); z-index: 100`.

Two viable approaches for your frozen death overlay: (a) a DOM element inside `.canvas-wrap` like `starvation-warning`, letting the canvas keep showing the frozen death behind it; or (b) draw directly on the canvas with `ctx.fillText` in `draw()`. Approach (a) reuses existing patterns and keeps the frozen board fully visible.

---

## 3. Keyboard / input handling

**Single listener:** `document.addEventListener('keydown', this.handleKeyPress.bind(this))` registered once in `setupEventListeners()` at `main.ts:634`. No `keyup` listener anywhere.

**Handler: `handleKeyPress(e)` â€” `main.ts:748-924`.** Order of handling:
- Ignores keys when focus is in an input/textarea/select (`main.ts:749-752`).
- **Already has "press a key when game over":** `Enter` restarts if `!gameStarted || isGameOver` (`main.ts:754-759`); `r`/`R` always restarts (`main.ts:761-764`).
- `Escape` â†’ back to menu (`main.ts:766-775`); `p`/`P` â†’ `togglePause()` (`main.ts:777-782`, guarded by `!isGameOver`); `b`/`B` â†’ toggle bot; `+`/`-` grid size; `o`/`O` â†’ overhead; `q`/`e` rotate; `t` auto-rotate pause; `[`/`]` perspective.
- **Guard line `main.ts:865`:** `if (this.isPaused || this.isGameOver) return` â€” movement keys below this are blocked once game is over. This is exactly where "any key to unfreeze" logic would sit (you'd want to intercept *before* this early-return, near the top of the handler).
- Direction handling: two-player WASD/arrows split at `main.ts:867-890`; single-player combined WASD+arrows keyMap at `main.ts:893-923`. First directional input starts the loop (`main.ts:916-920`).

`isValidDirection` at `main.ts:926-928`. `togglePause()` at `main.ts:930-938` (shows/hides `#pause` overlay).

For "any key pressed to unfreeze": add an early branch in `handleKeyPress` (before the `main.ts:865` guard) that, when a new "frozen death" state flag is set, consumes the keypress and dismisses the freeze (then either restarts or shows the game-over screen). Be aware `Enter`/`r` currently already restart when `isGameOver` is true (`main.ts:754-764`) â€” you'll want to reconcile that with a "freeze first, then any key" flow.

---

## 4. Loss reason / death cause

**A `deathCause` field already exists** (added for the starvation feature, git `acd663e`):
- Declared `main.ts:154`: `private deathCause: 'collision' | 'starved' = 'collision'`
- Reset to `'collision'` on new game: `main.ts:1104`
- Set to `'starved'` in single-player (`main.ts:1184`) and two-player (`main.ts:1276`)
- Consumed only to change the game-over title text: `main.ts:1814-1815` and `main.ts:1877-1878` (`'Starved!'` vs `'Game Over!'`), plus winner text nuance at `main.ts:1871-1875`.

It currently distinguishes only collision vs starvation â€” **not** wall vs self vs opponent. That finer detail is computed but discarded:
- **Two-player** `updateTwoPlayer` computes `p1HitsWall / p1HitsSelf / p1HitsP2 / headToHead` (`main.ts:1217-1229`) then collapses them into booleans passed to `endGameTwoPlayer(p1Dead, p2Dead)` (`main.ts:1231-1234`).
- **Single-player** uses `checkCollision(head)` â†’ `wouldCollide` (`main.ts:1520-1522`, `1444-1452`), which returns a plain boolean:
```ts
private wouldCollide(head, snake, blockedSet?) {
  if (!this.inBounds(head)) return true          // wall
  if (blockedSet) return blockedSet.has(`${head.x},${head.y}`)  // self
  return snake.some(segment => segment.x === head.x && segment.y === head.y)
}
```
So to report *how* you lost (wall vs self vs opponent vs starvation) you'd extend `deathCause` and capture the reason at the collision sites (`main.ts:1161-1164`, `1231-1234`, `1275-1278`).

---

## 5. HUD / status display

All HUD is DOM, in the `.game-info` bar (`index.html:80-88`): `#score`, `#p1-score`/`#p2-score`, `#level`, `#grid-size`, `#iron-snake-goal`, `#bot-status`. Updated by `updateUI()` at `main.ts:1721-1744`, which also calls `updateStarvationWarning()` (`main.ts:1750-1770`) to toggle the `#starvation-warning` overlay text. Controls hint line is `#controls-text` (`index.html:99`), set per-mode in `updateModeUI()` (`main.ts:1790-1796`).

---

## Implementation guidance for your feature

**The death path today (must change):** In `updateSinglePlayer` the collision is detected and `endGame()` is called *before* the head is added to the snake, so the fatal cell is never drawn (`main.ts:1161-1166`):
```ts
if (this.checkCollision(head)) {
  this.endGame()          // <-- immediately jumps to game-over screen
  return
}
this.snake.unshift(head)  // head only added AFTER the collision check
```
Same in `updateTwoPlayer`: `endGameTwoPlayer` is called at `main.ts:1231-1234` before `this.snake.unshift(head1)` at `main.ts:1237`. (The starvation deaths at `main.ts:1183-1187` and `1275-1279` happen *after* the head was added, so the body is current for those.)

`endGame()` (`main.ts:1801-1843`) and `endGameTwoPlayer()` (`main.ts:1845-1943`) both end with `this.showScreen('game-over')` (`main.ts:1842`, `1942`), and `showScreen` (`main.ts:2060-2070`) hides `#game` (canvas) and shows the `#game-over` DOM screen.

To "freeze showing how you lost": (1) before ending, draw the fatal position â€” e.g. push the colliding head into the objects list or draw a distinct collision marker so the overlap/wall hit is visible; (2) call `draw()` while still on the `#game` screen instead of immediately `showScreen('game-over')`; (3) set a new "awaiting keypress" flag and show a message overlay (mirror the `starvation-warning` DOM element inside `.canvas-wrap`, or `ctx.fillText` at the end of `draw()`); (4) in `handleKeyPress`, intercept before the `main.ts:865` guard so any key dismisses the freeze and proceeds to the existing game-over screen / restart. The `gameLoop` interval is already cleared inside `endGame`/`endGameTwoPlayer` (`main.ts:1804-1807`, `1848-1851`), so the board naturally stops animating; `isGameOver` (`main.ts:101`) is the existing flag, and `deathCause` (`main.ts:154`) is the natural place to carry the loss reason for your message.</result>

<result>I have everything I need. Here is my full report on the game state machine and loss/game-over lifecycle.

---

## Overview

The game is a single class `SnakeGame` in `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/main.ts` (instantiated at line 2088). "Screens" are HTML `<div class="screen">` elements in `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/index.html`; game state is a few boolean flags plus DOM visibility. There is **no state enum**.

---

## 1. Game states

No enum. State is three private booleans on the class, declared at `/Users/jeverest/projects/snake-game/src/main.ts`:

- Line 100: `private isPaused: boolean = false`
- Line 101: `private isGameOver: boolean = false`
- Line 102: `private gameStarted: boolean = false`
- Line 99: `private gameLoop: number | null = null` (also acts as a "loop running?" signal)

Plus `gameMode` ('single' | 'pvp' | 'bvb' | ...) with helper `isTwoSnakeMode()` at line 190 (`pvp || bvb`).

Screen switching is DOM-driven via `showScreen(screenId)` at line 2060:
```ts
private showScreen(screenId: string) {
  document.querySelectorAll('.screen:not(.overlay)').forEach(screen => {
    screen.classList.add('hidden')
  })
  document.getElementById(screenId)!.classList.remove('hidden')
  ...
}
```
Screens: `#menu`, `#leaderboards`, `#game`, `#game-over` (plain `.screen`), and `#pause` (`.screen overlay`) â€” see index.html lines 79, 101, 124. The main board `<canvas id="canvas">` lives **inside** `#game` (index.html lines 95-98).

Transitions: menu â†’ game via `startNewGame` (line 1033, ends with `showScreen('game')` at 1109); game â†’ game-over via `endGame`/`endGameTwoPlayer` (lines 1842, 1942); back to menu via `showScreen('menu')` (lines 675, 682, 773).

---

## 2. Game loop / tick

`setInterval`-based, handle stored in `this.gameLoop`:
```ts
this.gameLoop = window.setInterval(() => this.update(), this.gameSpeed)
```
Started at: line 919 (first directional keypress in single-player), line 1030 (`startLoopIfNeeded`, used by bot/two-snake modes and toggleBot), and re-created on speed changes at lines 1583 and 1673 (level advance / grid expansion clear then restart the interval).

Stopped via `clearInterval` + set to `null` at: line 670 (back-to-menu button), line 768 (Escape), line 1112 (startNewGame reset), line 1805 (`endGame`), line 1849 (`endGameTwoPlayer`).

The tick body, `update()` at line 1133:
```ts
private update() {
  if (this.isPaused || this.isGameOver) return   // <-- already gates on state
  if (this.isTwoSnakeMode()) this.updateTwoPlayer()
  else this.updateSinglePlayer()
}
```

(Separately, `autoRotateRafId` at line 162 is a `requestAnimationFrame` handle used only for the cosmetic board auto-rotation â€” not the game tick.)

---

## 3. Loss detection

**Single player** â€” `updateSinglePlayer` (line 1143): computes new `head`, then line 1161:
```ts
if (this.checkCollision(head)) {
  this.endGame()
  return
}
```
`checkCollision` (line 1520) â†’ `wouldCollide` (line 1444): returns true if `!inBounds(head)` (wall) OR the head cell is in the snake (self). Starvation death at lines 1183-1187: `if (this.movesSinceFood1 >= this.starvationLimit()) { this.deathCause = 'starved'; this.endGame() }`.

**Two player** â€” `updateTwoPlayer` (line 1193): computes both heads, then lines 1217-1234 evaluate wall/self/other-snake/head-to-head for each snake:
```ts
const p1Dead = p1HitsWall || p1HitsSelf || p1HitsP2 || headToHead
const p2Dead = p2HitsWall || p2HitsSelf || p2HitsP1 || headToHead
if (p1Dead || p2Dead) { this.endGameTwoPlayer(p1Dead, p2Dead); return }
```
Two-player starvation at lines 1272-1279.

The moment of loss, `endGame()` (line 1801): sets `isGameOver = true`, `stopAutoRotate()`, `clearInterval(gameLoop)` + null, saves high score, populates the `#game-over` DOM (title based on `deathCause`, line 1814), optionally prompts leaderboard, then `showScreen('game-over')` (line 1842). `endGameTwoPlayer` (line 1845) is the same pattern plus winner resolution.

---

## 4. Multiplayer / bots

There are at most **two** snakes: `snake`/`snakeSet` (P1) and `snake2`/`snakeSet2` (P2). Modes: `single`, `pvp`, `bvb` (bot-vs-bot). `isTwoSnakeMode()` = pvp or bvb (line 190). In single mode a bot (`botEnabled`, line 105) can drive the single snake.

There is **no free-for-all where snakes die independently while others continue.** In two-snake mode the game ends the instant *either* snake dies â€” line 1231 `if (p1Dead || p2Dead)` calls `endGameTwoPlayer` immediately and returns. `endGameTwoPlayer` (line 1845) determines outcome from the two dead-flags: both dead â†’ Draw, else the survivor wins (lines 1856-1868).

A bot-driven snake dies through the exact same code path as a human â€” bots only choose a direction (`chooseBotDirection` line 1287, `chooseBotDirectionForPlayer` line 1310); they do not alter the death lifecycle. The only bot-specific effect on game-over is leaderboard eligibility: `botEverActive` (line 108) disqualifies a single-player run from the human leaderboard (line 1829), and bvb auto-records both bots without a name prompt (lines 1896-1916).

---

## 5. Restart flow

Triggered by:
- "Play Again" button â†’ `startNewGame()` (event listener line 668).
- "Menu" button â†’ clears loop, `showScreen('menu')` (lines 669-676).
- `Enter` key while `!gameStarted || isGameOver` â†’ `startNewGame()` (lines 754-758).
- `r`/`R` key (any time) â†’ `startNewGame()` (lines 761-764).
- `Escape` â†’ clears loop, disables bot, `showScreen('menu')` (lines 766-775).

`startNewGame(mode?)` (line 1033) does the full reset: re-seats snakes, resets scores, and at lines 1091-1106 resets `isPaused=false`, `isGameOver=false`, `gameStarted=false`, `gameStartTime=0`, `movesSinceFood*=0`, `deathCause='collision'`, etc.; then `spawnFood()`, `showScreen('game')` (1109), clears the loop (1112-1113), and only auto-starts the loop for two-snake/bot modes (1119-1121). Human single-player waits for the first arrow/WASD key (line 916-920) to start.

---

## 6. What's available at the moment of death (most relevant to your feature)

**State is NOT reset on death.** `endGame`/`endGameTwoPlayer` never touch `this.snake`, `this.snake2`, `this.food`, or the scores â€” those are only cleared later inside `startNewGame` (lines 1050-1106). So the losing positions survive in memory until the next new game. There is one field naming the cause, `deathCause: 'collision' | 'starved'` (line 154), but there is **no stored collision coordinate**.

However, three important caveats mean the losing frame is not currently *shown* as "how you died":

1. **The fatal head is never added to the snake.** In `updateSinglePlayer`, `checkCollision(head)` is checked at line 1161 *before* `this.snake.unshift(head)` at line 1166. On death the function returns early, so the snake array still holds the previous tick's body â€” its head is one cell short of the wall/segment it struck. The colliding cell exists only in the local `head` variable and is discarded. Two-player is identical: heads computed at 1206-1214, death check at 1231, `unshift` at 1237-1240.

2. **`draw()` is not called in the death path.** `draw()` (line 563) runs only at the end of a *successful* tick (line 1189 single, line 1281 two-player). So the canvas bitmap holds the pre-collision frame, not the collision itself.

3. **`showScreen('game-over')` hides the board.** `#game` is a plain `.screen`, so it gets `display: none` (style.css lines 84-86) when the game-over screen appears â€” the canvas, even with its last frame, becomes invisible.

### How "freeze until keypress" fits the existing control flow

The freeze half is essentially already there: `endGame` stops the interval and `update()` early-returns on `isGameOver` (line 1134), so nothing advances. What's missing is (a) rendering the death and (b) keeping the board visible while waiting for a key.

The codebase already contains the exact pattern you'd reuse â€” the **pause overlay** (`#pause`, index.html line 124, `class="screen hidden overlay"`; style.css lines 88-97 make `.overlay` a `position: fixed`, semi-transparent, full-screen layer). `togglePause()` (line 930) simply toggles that overlay's `hidden` class *without* calling `showScreen`, so `#game` stays visible underneath. A "you died â€” press a key" prompt could be implemented the same way: an `.overlay` shown on top of the still-visible `#game` canvas.

Concretely, the natural insertion points are:
- Split `endGame`/`endGameTwoPlayer` (lines 1801, 1845) so the interval-stop + a *death render* happen first, but the `showScreen('game-over')` transition is deferred. To render the actual collision you'd push the fatal `head` into the snake (or draw a distinct collision marker at `head`) and call `draw()` before the freeze.
- Add a new phase flag (e.g. `awaitingDeathDismissal`) alongside `isGameOver`, and handle the dismissing keypress in `handleKeyPress` (line 748). Note it already has `Enter` (754-758) and `r`/`R` (761-764) branches that call `startNewGame()` while `isGameOver` is true â€” those would need reconciling so the *first* post-death key reveals the game-over screen (via `showScreen('game-over')`) instead of immediately restarting.
- Because `update()` already bails while `isGameOver` is true and the interval is cleared, no additional loop-suppression is required during the freeze.

Key files/lines to touch: `endGame` (1801-1843), `endGameTwoPlayer` (1845-1943), `updateSinglePlayer` collision branch (1161-1164), `updateTwoPlayer` collision branch (1231-1234), `handleKeyPress` (748-924, esp. 754-764), `showScreen` (2060), `draw` (563); overlay CSS at style.css 88-97; `#game-over` markup at index.html 101-123.</result>

Both agents converged on a clear picture. Let me read the critical code sections myself to verify details before designing.

I now have a thorough understanding of the death flow. Let me pressure-test the design with a Plan agent while I clarify a few forks with you.

Now let me clarify the key forks with you while the plan agent works.

Both the plan agent's design and your answers align. Let me write the final plan.

The plan is written. Here's the summary:

## Freeze-on-death with loss reason

**The problem:** Today the moment a snake dies, the game jumps straight to the Game Over screen, which hides the canvas â€” so you never see *how* you died. The fatal head cell isn't even drawn (collision is checked before the head is added to the snake).

**The fix:** Insert a "death freeze" phase in front of the existing end-game logic. When any snake dies, the board stays visible and frozen, the fatal collision cell is highlighted in an alarm color, a banner names the reason ("Hit the wall!", "Ran into itself!", etc.), and the game waits for any key before advancing to the normal Game Over screen.

**Key design choice:** `endGame`/`endGameTwoPlayer` stay untouched â€” they become the "finalize" step, deferred behind a stored callback. This preserves all the winner/leaderboard/high-score logic verbatim and keeps the change low-risk.

Per your answers: any key â†’ the existing Game Over screen; highlight *plus* text banner; applies to all modes (human, bot, bot-vs-bot).

**Touches 3 files:** `src/main.ts` (new fields, `beginDeathFreeze`, banner methods, four death sites, death-cell rendering in `draw()`, an ack branch at the top of `handleKeyPress`), `index.html` (a `#death-banner` overlay mirroring the existing starvation-warning pattern), and `src/style.css` (a `.death-banner` rule).

The plan also nails the tricky edge cases: out-of-bounds wall cells clipping off-canvas (clamp to the last valid head + draw a sliver past the edge), Iron Snake masked-void deaths, both-snakes-dying-same-tick, and persisting the highlight across resize/rotate redraws.

The plan is complete and written to the plan file. Requesting your approval to proceed.

Plan approved. Let me set up task tracking and start implementing.

I'll simplify: rather than a write-only structured `deathReason` field, I'll store the composed banner message directly (computed at each death site where full per-snake context is available). Let me revise the fields.

Now let me wire each death site. Single collision first:

Let me verify the `drawBlock`/`drawBlockShadow` signatures before adding the render block:

Now I'll append the death-cell rendering after the block-drawing loop in `draw()`:

Task 4 done. Now Task 5: the ack branch in `handleKeyPress`. Let me find the current location of the INPUT guard:

Task 5 done. Now Task 6: the banner DOM + CSS.

## What changed

When any snake loses, the game now **freezes on the board showing how it died** â€” the fatal cell is highlighted in an amber "impact" block on the still-visible board, and a banner names the reason ("Hit the wall!", "Ran into itself!", "Starved!", or per-snake text in two-player). Pressing **any key** dismisses the freeze and advances to the normal Game Over screen.

**`src/main.ts`**
- New fields beside `deathCause`: `deathMessage`, `deathCells`, `awaitingDeathAck`, `pendingFinalize` (reset in `startNewGame`, plus `hideDeathBanner()`).
- New `beginDeathFreeze(finalize)` â€” stops the loop, renders the frozen frame, shows the banner, and stores the deferred `endGame`/`endGameTwoPlayer` call. The existing end-game functions are untouched and reused verbatim as the "finalize" step, so all winner/leaderboard/high-score logic is preserved.
- All four death sites (single collision, single starvation, two-player collision, two-player starvation) now route through the freeze, capturing the reason and fatal cell(s).
- `draw()` highlights the death cell(s) on top of everything, with an off-grid clamp fallback so wall deaths stay visible.
- An acknowledgement branch at the top of `handleKeyPress` (above Enter/r/Escape) dismisses on any key, ignoring auto-repeat and bare modifiers.
- `showDeathBanner()` / `hideDeathBanner()` next to the starvation-warning logic.

**`index.html`** â€” a `#death-banner` overlay inside `.canvas-wrap` (same pattern as the starvation warning).
**`src/style.css`** â€” a centered, pulsing `.death-banner` alarm style.

## What changed

Two adjustments to the freeze behavior:

1. **`beginDeathFreeze` no longer stops auto-rotate.** In bot/bot-vs-bot modes (where auto-rotate runs), the board keeps spinning during the freeze â€” a proper death cam. The deferred `endGame`/`endGameTwoPlayer` still stops it when the freeze ends.

2. **The acknowledgement branch in `handleKeyPress` now whitelists the camera keys** â€” rotate (`q`/`e`), auto-rotate toggle (`t`), overhead (`o`), and perspective (`[` `]`). These fall through to their normal view handlers instead of dismissing, so you can rotate/tilt the frozen board to inspect the collision from any angle. Every *other* key still dismisses to the Game Over screen. Since `draw()` re-renders the highlighted death cell on each redraw, the collision highlight persists as you rotate.

I included overhead and perspective alongside rotate/auto-rotate since they're the same class of pure camera control â€” excluding them would mean pressing `o` mid-inspection accidentally ends the freeze. If you'd rather have only `q`/`e`/`t` fall through and let `o`/`[`/`]` dismiss, that's a one-line tweak to the whitelist.

**Verification:** `tsc` + build pass; the CDP driver confirms that pressing `q`/`e`/`t`/`o`/`[`/`]` during the freeze keeps the banner up and Game Over hidden, while a non-camera key still advances â€” plus all the earlier single-player and two-player flow checks.
